coredata.dc:title: Supersensitive detection of chloramphenicol with an EIS method based on molecularly imprinted polypyrrole at UiO-66 and CDs modified electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Bu, Liyin
Chen, Xiaohui
Song, Qingyuan
Jiang, Ding
Shan, Xueling
Wang, Wenchang
Chen, Zhidong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a supersensitive electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis method combined the molecularly imprinted polypyrrole (MIP) which electropolymerized on the composites of zirconium-based metal organic framework (Uio-66) and carbon dots (CDs) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was developed for chloramphenicol (CAP) determination. The integration of Uio-66 and CDs effectively promoted the sensitivity of the proposed MIP senor by improving the surface area and the charge transfer of electrode. By extraction of CAP template, the specific sites were formed on the MIP surface which can rewrap the CAP target molecules. The CAP determination was based on the charge transfer difference (△Rct) before and after the MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE rebinding the CAP. The results showed that △Rct responded good linearity to the logarithm of CAP concetration in the range of 0.1 – 100 pmol/L, with a low detection limit of 0.061 pmol/L (S/N = 3), exhibiting high sensitivity and specificity for the determination of CAP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supersensitive detection
Electrochemical impedance analysis method
Uio-66@CDs composites
Charge transfer difference
Chloramphenicol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The CV behaviors of the modified electrodes
The EIS behaviors of the modified electrodes

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22102012 ) and Changzhou Science and Technology Plan Applied Basic Research Project ( CJ20210129 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of UiO-66, CDs, and UiO-66@CDs. (b) XRD patterns of UiO-66 and UiO-66@CDs. The SEM morphologies of (c) UiO-66/GCE, (d) UiO-66@CDs/GCE, (e) PMIP/UiO-66@CDs/GCE, and (f) MIP/UiO-66@CDs/GCE magnified 30,000 times.

(A) The CV curves of different modified electrodes in 0.1 mol/L KCl containing 5 mmol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. (B) The CV curves of MIP modified electrodes before and after rebinding CAP in 0.1 mol/L KCl containing 5 mmol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. The embedded CAP concentration in PMIP is 1 × 10–4 mol/L, and the concentration of rebinded CAP in MIP is 1 × 10–8 mol/L.

Nyquist plots of the EIS for different modified electrodes in 0.1 mol/L KCl solution containing 5 mmol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. The embedded CAP concentration in PMIP is 1 × 10–4 mol/L, and the concentration of rebinded CAP in MIP is 1 × 10–8 mol/L.
(A) EIS measurements for various concentrations of CAP at MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE. (B) the calibration curve for the determination of CAP.
Comparison of the reported electrochemical method for CAP detection.
Determination of CAP in real samples.

head.ce:title: Supersensitive detection of chloramphenicol with an EIS method based on molecularly imprinted polypyrrole at UiO-66 and CDs modified electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, No.1 Gehu Road, Changzhou 213164, Jiangsu Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, No.1 Gehu Road, Changzhou 213164, Jiangsu Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Changzhou Institute of Technology, No.666 Liaohe Road, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Changzhou Institute of Technology, No.666 Liaohe Road, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213164, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a supersensitive electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis method combined the molecularly imprinted polypyrrole (MIP) which electropolymerized on the composites of zirconium-based metal organic framework (Uio-66) and carbon dots (CDs) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was developed for chloramphenicol (CAP) determination. The integration of Uio-66 and CDs effectively promoted the sensitivity of the proposed MIP senor by improving the surface area and the charge transfer of electrode. By extraction of CAP template, the specific sites were formed on the MIP surface which can rewrap the CAP target molecules. The CAP determination was based on the charge transfer difference (△Rct) before and after the MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE rebinding the CAP. The results showed that △Rct responded good linearity to the logarithm of CAP concetration in the range of 0.1 – 100 pmol/L, with a low detection limit of 0.061 pmol/L (S/N = 3), exhibiting high sensitivity and specificity for the determination of CAP.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that chloramphenicol (CAP) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic which has inhibitory effect on Gram-positive and Gram-negative infections [1]. Meanwhile, due to its features of low cost, high efficiency, strong activity, and easy to obtain, CAP has widely been applied in aquaculture to prevent diseases [2]. However, the CAP residues in food producing animals can cause harm to human health through combining with human mitochondria and inhibiting the protein synthesis of human mitochondria, and thus the use of CAP in aquaculture is strictly forbidden thereby [3]. Hence, quantitative and sensitive analysis of CAP in food samples are of significant importance [4]. Up to now, a wide variety of analytical methodologies has been developed for CAP determination, such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [5], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry/mass spectrometry [6], and solid phase extraction [7]. However, those methods have some shortcomings of time-consuming pretreatment, expensive instruments, and skilled operators [8]. The electrochemical techniques, e.g. cyclic voltammetry [9,10], differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) [11], and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) [12], have many advantages including low cost, fast response, and excellent analytical performance, arousing great attention in analysis researches [13]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is generally used as an effective characterization method by motoring the charge transfer of the electrochemical systems [14], and meanwhile it has been proved as a promising means in trace detection recently [15].

As so far, various electrochemical sensors based on valid modification, such as nanomaterials [16], molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) [17], and compositions [18], have been developed for CAP recognition and selective detection. MIP, which is synthesized by copolymerization of functional monomers and cross-linker in the presence of the template molecules, can effectively recognize the target molecules by providing the recognition sites with distinct shape, size, and chemical functionality [19]. Although having attracted great attentions owing to the unique recognition ability and selective efficiency, the MIPs usually have many shortcomings such as slow diffusion of analytes in material, poor electrochemical activity, and low adhesion to the electrode surface [20]. In this regard, several nanomaterials, such as carbon-based nanomaterials [21,22] and metallic nanoparticles [23–25], have been used to integrated into MIP sensors to increase surface area and favor charge transfer. Roushanil et al., [26] developed a hybrid-MIP receptor for CAP analysis. The 3-aminomethyl pyridine functionalized graphene oxide (3-ampy-RGO) and silver nanoparticle (AgNPs) were subsequently coated on the GCE surface, and the CAP complex-amino-aptamer was used as the templates and electropolymerization with resorcinol. Despite the detection limit of the provide MIP was obtained as low as 0.3 pm, the fabrication was relatively tedious. Yang et al. [17] used a novel monomer of 3-hexadecyl-1-vinylimidazolium chloride (C16VimCl) synthetized by 1-vinylimidazole and 1-chlorohexadecane for MIP construction. The multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) was utilized for promoting the sensitivity of CAP determination, and the detection limit was 0.1 nm. The preparation protocol of the functional polymer was complicated, and the obtained quantity of MIP was not enough as expected. Therefore, a facile and sensitive MIP sensor for CAP detection is still in need of exploration.

As a promising conducting polymer, polypyrrole (PPy) is widely used in electrochemical imprinting due to its high conductivity and chemical inertness [27]. Ye et al. [28] proposed an imprinted PPy electrochemical sensor based on CoFe-metal organic framework (MOFs)/Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) for norfloxacin determination, the analytical sensitivity has significantly been improved due to the high surface area extended by CoFe-MOFs and the favorable electron transfer ability of AuNPs. Uio-66 is a metal organic framework (MOF) material prepared with Zr4+ as the metal ions and the carboxylates as the ligand, which is a porous crystal structure of high specific surface area and strong adsorption properties [29]. Liang et al. [30] constructed a UiO-66-NH2@MIP sorbent used in high performance liquid chromatography, which exhibited great sensitivity for aflatoxin detection because of the amino-functionalized Uio-66 provided high surface to extend the imprinted membrane and increase the imprinted cavities. Unfortunately, the electrical conductivity of Uio-66 is poor [31], which limits its application in electrochemical sensors. Carbon dots (CDs), a tiny particle smaller than 10 nm which exhibits several attractive advantages including excellent conductivity, inexpensive, chemical stability, and non-toxicity, have been widely applied to improve the sensitivity of electrochemical sensors [32–34]. Therefore, the trace detection could be realized by the combination of CDs and Uio-66. Although various materials have been used to improve the sensitivity of MIP sensors, the composites of Uio-66@CDs have not been applied for MIP senor construction as so far.

In the present work, we developed a novel EIS method combined MIP sensor for supersensitive detection of CAP. The PPy acted as the functional MIP for CAP imprinting and the Uio-66 was used to increase the MIP carried amount. In addition, the carbon dots (CDs) were combined with Uio-66 for improving the conductivity of Uio-66. Ascribing to the high surface area of Uio-66 and the good conductivity of CDs, the prepared MIP/Uio-66@CDs modified glassy carbon electrode (MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE) showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity for CAP detection. Under optimized conditions, the detection limit was obtained as 0.061 pmol/L (S/N = 3).

Zirconium chloride, citric acid, ethanediamine, methanol, ethanol, and potassium chloride were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Terephthalic acid, pyrrole, norfloxacin, enrofloxacin, dimetridazole, benzimidazole, furaclin, macrodantin, and thiamphenicol were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ciprofloxacin, potassium ferricyanide, and potassium ferrocyanide trihydrate were purchased from Energy Chemical. CAP, kanamycin, and acetic acid were obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All drugs were analytical grades and the solutions were prepared in ultrapure water (>18 MΩ).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and EIS measurement were carried out on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation in a traditional three-electrode cell consisting of modified GCE (φ = 3 mm) as the working electrode, a platinum foil as the auxiliary electrode, and a Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded on the JEOL scanning electron microscope (JSM-6360LA, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed by the Nicolet In10 (Thermo Fisher, USA) and the X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded by the Max-2000 (Rigaku Co., Ltd., Japan). The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were obtained using a FEI (Tecnai G2 F20) transmission electron microscope. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was performed by the AXIS-Ultra DLD (Shimazu, Japan).

The UiO-66 was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis according to a reported method with some modifications [35]. Briefly, 1.0 mmol/L ZrCl4 and 1.0 mmol/L terephthalic acid were dissolved in 38.7 mL DMF to form a mixed solution. Then, the dissolved mixture was subsequently transferred to 100 mL PTFE autoclave and heated at 120 ℃ for 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid products in the mixture were collected by successively washing by DMF and methanol and centrifugation under 8000 rpm for 15 min, and then dried in a vacuum at 120 ℃ for 24 h.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared according to the previous work of our group [36]. In brief, 0.5 mol/L monohydrate citric acid and 0.5 mol/L ethanediamine were firstly dissolved in 10 mL ultra-pure water. After stirring for 10 min, the formed homogeneous solution was transferred to 50 mL PTFE autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was then dialysis in ultrapure water by a dialysis membrane. Finally, the brownness carbon powders were obtained after centrifugation and freeze-drying. 1.0 mg of the prepared Uio-66 and 1.0 mg of CDs were dispersed in 2.0 mL of DMF and ultra-sonicated for 30 min to obtain Uio-66@CDs suspension.

Prior to use, the GCE was polished successively on chamois leather with 0.3 and 0.05 μm Al2O3 slurry, followed by ultrasonication in a HNO3/water mixture (v/v, 1:1), ethanol, and water. 5 μL of the suspension was dropped onto the GCE surface by microsyringe and dried under an infrared lamp to obtain modified Uio-66@CDs/GCE.

The fabrication processes of MIP/UiO-66@CDs/GCE are shown in Scheme 1 , including electropolymerization and template extraction. A conventional three-electrode system was set up for electrochemical polymerization, consisting of a Uio-66@CDs/GCE as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum foil as the auxiliary electrode. The electropolymerization was performed in 0.05 mol/L phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing 1.0 × 10−3 mol/L pyrrole and 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L CAP by CV from 0 to 0.8 V for 20 cycles at 50 mV/s.

The CAP templates in the PPy film were extracted by the mixture of methanol and acetic acid (v/v, 9:1) for 12 min at room temperature to obtain the MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE. The pre-extracted PPy modified electrode was noted as PMIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE.

The MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE was used as the working electrode for determination of CAP, and the auxiliary and the reference electrodes were the same as in the electropolymerization procedure. The determination of CAP was carried out by EIS, in which the potential was 0.2 V, the amplitude of the applied sine wave potential was 10 mV, and the frequency range was set from 105 to 1 Hz. The solution of 0.1 mol/L KCl containing 5 mmol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] was used as the supporting electrolyte to obtain the EIS response.

The CAP determination was based on the difference of the charge transfer resistance (Rct) which was given by the semicircle diameter of EIS results, △Rct = Rct – Rct0, where Rct is the charge transfer resistance value of MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE after rebinding various concentration of CAP, while Rct0 represents the semicircle diameter of MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE in the supporting electrolyte without CAP.

The characterization of MIP/UiO-66@CDs have been exhibited in Fig. 1 . The FT-IR spectra of Uio-66, CDs, and the composites of Uio-66@CDs are shown in Fig. 1a. For Uio-66, the broad peak showed at 3390 cm−1 was ascribed to the –OH stretching vibrations, while the bands at 1582 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1 were related to the stretching modes of carboxylate group, and the sharp peak located at 750 cm−1 was attributed to Zr-O2. As for CDs, an absorption peak at approximately 1440 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of C-N, and the broad peaks at 1650 and 1560 cm−1 were ascribed to the vibrations of C = O from carboxyl group. The characteristic peak locations of Uio-66 and CDs were close to each other, due to the existence of carboxylates in Uio-66 and CDs. It can clearly be seen that the Uio-66@CDs showed both the absorption bands from CDs and Uio-66, which confirmed the formation of Uio-66@CDs. The XRD pattern of the prepared Uio-66 was exact matched with the calculated XRD [37] (Fig. 1b). After integration of the CDs, the XRD pattern of Uio-66@CDs was compatible with that of Uio-66 despite the peak intensities of Uio-66@CDs were relatively low, further verifying the successfully loading of CDs on Uio-66.

The chemical composition of UiO-66@CDs is investigated by XPS (Fig. S1). The overall survey spectrum (Fig. S1a) notified the presence of Zr, C, N, and O in UiO-66@CDs composite. In C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1b), three peaks were observed in 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.8 eV, which corresponded to the C–C, C-O, and C = O groups, respectively. The peak at 400.3 eV of N 1 s (Fig. S1c) was ascribed to C-N in CDs. For O 1 s XPS spectrum shown in Fig. S1d, the high-resolution O 1 s peak could be fitted into three peaks centered at 529.8 eV, 531.5 eV, and 532.7 eV, which were ascribed to Zr-O-Zr, C-O-Zr, and –COOH groups. The Zr 3d peak could be de-convoluted into Zr 3d5/2 at 182.7 eV, Zr 3d3/2 at 185.1 eV (fig. S1e), agreeing with previous research [38].

HRTEM images of Uio-66, CDs, and UiO-66@CDs are shown in Fig. 2 . It is shown in Fig. S2a that the Uio-66 exhibited a hexahedral configuration and its particle dimeter was about 50–100 nm. The CDs exhibited tiny and circular which diameter was smaller than 10 nm (Fig. S2b). The interplanar spacing of CDs was 0.33 nm, which indicated that the carbon dots have the properties of crystalline graphite [38]. From the images of UiO-66@CDs shown in Fig. S3c, the CDs uniformly dispersed on the Uio-66 surfaces.

The morphologies of Uio-66, Uio-66@CDs, and MIP/Uio-66@CDs were characterized by SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 1c-f. UiO-66 had a regular polyhedral structure with a particle diameter of about 50–100 nm (Fig. 1c), which is consistent with the reported literature [25]. When the UiO-66 integrated with CDs, the granular particles became irregular and larger (Fig. 1d). After electropolymerization of PPy-CAP, a dense and uniform surface can be observed on the surface of Uio-66@CDs/GCE (Fig. 1e). After templates extraction, the three-dimensional cavities were distributed on the MIP surface as seen in Fig. 1f. The results from the SEM images indicated that the MIP was successfully fabricated on the Uio-66@CDs/GCE surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CV behaviors of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behaviors of 5 mmol/L Fe(CN)6 3–/4– at different modified electrodes are shown in Fig. 2. As demonstrated in Fig. 2A, a pair of reversible redox peaks showed at the bare GCE. The peak intensities decreased after the modification of Uio-66 on GCE, owing to the poor conductivity and the lack of signal transition capacity of Uio-66. When CDs were integrated with Uio-66, the peak currents exhibited a remarkable increase. However, the redox peaks disappeared after the PPy-CAP formed at Uio-66@CDs/GCE, due to the poor electron transferability of the compact membrane. After extraction of the templates, the cavities were formed which facilitated the probe molecules of Fe(CN)6 3–/4– enter to the electrode surface and resulted in the increase of current intensities (cf. Fig. 2B). Meanwhile, the formed cavities can incubate the CAP molecules not only due to the specific size and shape but also the hydrogen bond (Scheme 1). Therefore, after rebinding 1 × 10–8 mol/L CAP, the redox currents of MIP/Uio-66@CDs decreased again because of the target molecules reenter the cavities and blocked the charge transfer. Obviously, the prepared MIP sensor can effectively recognize the CAP molecules. However, the current intensities of low concentration of CAP are rather low and difficult to detect by CV method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The EIS behaviors of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The EIS techniques are sensitive to the charge transfer resistance changes of the modified electrodes, which can be observed from the semicircle of the EIS curves. The anodic of the modified electrodes and the oxidation of CAP were observed at potential of approximately 0.2 V (Fig. 2), 0.2 V was chosen as the initial potential for EIS measurement. The Nyquist plots of EIS of different modified electrodes are shown in Fig. 3 , it can be seen that the obvious changes of charge transfer resistance for the MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE under different circumstances. Before template extraction, the semicircle diameter of PMIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE reached to 13.7 kΩ due to the dense polymer membrane inhibited the charge transfer. While the CAP templates were extracted, the semicircle diameter of MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE remarkablely decreased to 654 Ω owing to the formed cavities play the role as the ion transport channel. After rebinding the 1 × 10–8 mol/L CAP, the charge transfer resistance of MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE re-increased to 5451 Ω because of the CAP molecules occupied into some cavities and reduced the ion transference numbers. The trace concentration of target molecule can result in the apparent changes of charge transfer resistance. Thus, by monitoring the semicircle diameter changes of various concentration of CAP at MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE, the very low concentration of CAP can readily be detected by EIS analytical method.

Fig. S3 exhibited the resistance differences (△Rct) of the modified electrodes of MIP/GCE, MIP/Uio-66/GCE, and MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE before and after rebinding CAP. Before rebinding, the charge transfer resistance of MIP/Uio-66/GCE was largest which was caused by the poor conductivity of Uio-66, and that of MIP/GCE and MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE were close to each other which was in concordance with the CV results. However, after rebinding of CAP, the semicircle diameter of MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE was larger than that of MIP/GCE, which was probably due to the existence of Uio-66@CDs limit the charge transmission rate. The △Rct values for MIP/GCE, MIP/Uio-66/GCE, and MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE were evaluated as 3143 Ω, 4289 Ω, and 4797 Ω, respectively. The participation of Uio-66@CDs in MIP sensor can significantly improve the sensitivity of CAP detection.

The detection sensitivity mainly relates to the charge transfer difference. That is, the charge transfer resistance of MIP/Uio-66@CDs before CAP rebinding should be small but that of the MIP/Uio-66@CDs after incubation of CAP ought to large enough. To obtain the optimal sensitivity, the effect parameters of scan cycles of electropolymerization, the extraction time, and accumulation duration were investigated which as shown in Fig. S4.

The thickness of electropolymerized PPy is closely associated with the scan cycles of CV. The charge transfer at electrode-solution interface would be partially hindered by the thick PPy film; on the other hand, the thicker PPy could form more cavities as the recognition site on the surface of the MIP sensor. As can been seen in Fig. S4A, with increasing of scan cycles, the semicircle diameter increased and reached its maximum at 20 cycles and almost kept unchanged at 25 and 30 cycles. Thus, the scan cycle of electropolymerization was chosen as 25 cycles.

Templates extraction is crucial for MIP fabrication, which causes the formation of special recognition sites on the electrode surface. The templates should be completely removed in order not to influence the EIS response. The effect of extraction duration can be seen in Fig. S4B, the semicircle dimeter gradually decreased with increasing of extraction time and remained unchanged from 12 to 18 min, indicating the CAP templates were completely removed. Therefore, the templates extraction duration was set as 12 min.

The accumulation process can promote the adsorption amount of target molecules on the electrode surface and further improve the analytical sensitivity. The effect of accumulation time on the charge transfer resistance was investigated over a time period of 3 to 18 min. As is evidence in Fig. S4C, the semicircle diameter increased as the CAP reoccupied to the cavities and inhibited the charge transfer of electron-solution interface. The charge transfer resistance reached its maximum at 12 min which was chosen as the accumulation time.

Under the optimal conditions, the MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE was used to determinate the CAP. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the semicircle diameter was gradually declined with decrease of CAP concentration, and △Rct values were found to be linear with the logarithm of CAP concentrations (Fig. 4B). The equation for the resulting calibration plot was obtained as △Rct (kΩ) = 1.2 (±0.03) lgC (pmol/L) + 2.27 (±0.04), with a correlation coefficient of r = 0.9960. The limit of detection was obtained as 0.061 pmol/L (S/N = 3). Compared with the most commonly used methods which were list in Table 1 , our proposed MIP sensor combined the EIS analytical method exhibited a satisfactory detection limit.

To obtain the selectivity of the sensor, the EIS responses of the sensor to other mycotoxins as enrofloxacin, norfoxacin, ciprofiloxacin, kanamycin, dimetridazole, benzimidazole, furaclin, macrodantin, and thiamphenicol were observed as well (cf. Fig. S5). The ΔRct for CAP is far large than the other antibiotics which are hard to enter the recognition cavities due to their sizes and shapes are not compatible to the specific recognition cavities. Therefore, the proposed MIP-based sensor exhibits good selectivity to CAP.

The MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE was used to detect CAP by repeating the process of template extraction and rebinding to examine the repeatability. The results showed that the △Rct values kept almost unchanged after the third use. At the fifth use, about 86% of the was sustained, which indicated the proposed sensor has great reusability (Fig S6a). The reproducibility of the proposed MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE sensor was evaluated by comparing the △Rct value of five sensors prepared under the same protocol. The results showed a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.6%, indicating good reproducibility of the proposed sensor for CAP detection. (Fig.S6b). It was found that the signal for CAP on the sensor remained almost unchanged when the sensor was used again one month later and kept almost 84% after 45 days, i.e. the stability of sensor is satisfactory.

Recover testing was performed to demonstrate the validity of the proposed method. The tap water was used as the real samples. As shown in Table 2 , the obtained recoveries of three samples were found to be 96.0–103.6%. The results indicated that the proposed method was reliable and able to be employed in the analysis of the real samples.
4. Conclusion.

In the present work, a supersensitive EIS method combined the MIP sensor was fabricated for CAP determination. The Uio-66 has high surface area and the CDs possess good conductivity, and the composites of Uio-66@CDs effectively improved the MIP loading amount and the charge transfer. Under the selected conditions, the EIS responses of MIP/Uio-66@CDs/GCE showed good linearity in the range of 0.1 – 100 pmol/L of CAP concentrations. Meanwhile, the results presented a low detection limit as 0.061 pmol/L and good selectivity and reproducibility.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22102012) and Changzhou Science and Technology Plan Applied Basic Research Project (CJ20210129).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107459.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptasensors as the future of antibiotics test kits-a case study of the aptamer application in the chloramphenicol detection
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Khoshbin, A. Verdian, M.R. Housaindokht, M. Izadyar, Z. Rouhbakhsh, Aptasensors as the future of antibiotics test kits-a case study of the aptamer application in the chloramphenicol detection, Biosens. Bioelectron. 122 (2018) 263-283.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ generated molecularlyimprinted material for chloramphenicol electrochemical sensing in waters down to the nanomolar level
Sensor Actuat. B-Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.R. Cardoso, A.P.M. Tavares, M.G.F. Sales, In-situ generated molecularlyimprinted material for chloramphenicol electrochemical sensing in waters down to the nanomolar level, Sensor Actuat. B-Chem. 256 (2018) 420-428.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A procedure for the control of the residual chloramphenicol (laevomycetin) in food products of animal origin
J. Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.I. Polyanskikh, A.G. Polonevich, L.L. Belysheva, E.M. Rakhman'ko, S.M. Leshchev, A procedure for the control of the residual chloramphenicol (laevomycetin) in food products of animal origin. J. Anal. Chem. 74 (2019) 601-608 .
